Comment: Tyson could have sparked him out if he wanted. He didn't want to, Mike wanted the challenge he wanted to see what he is still able to do, and to see how much he could withstand. Paul was an interesting match for him, someone who Mike maybe sees abit of himself in with how he's travelling through his boxing era. The age difference alone should be enough to give it away that it was never about knocking eachother out but more about the experience and obviously the money in it.... but they had to give the win to someone. Mike let him have it, if you didn't notice,  with Jake and all his confidence, Mike brought him down a peg or two when he got his few digs at Jake in the first 2 rounds.... he humbled him and taught him a life lesson that he will carry with him for the rest of his days. They don't care what anyone thinks of it... it was a moment for them where they both were beneficial for eachother..... plus it's entertainment one way or another. Weather you are open minded about it or moaning about the quality it's getting the talking going.... it's making the fuss that they knew would be made.... and they took more from that fight than what people realise because alot of people can't see past the basic visuals of their entertainment from watching. Comment:
It’s almost laughable to compare Mike Tyson arguably one of the greatest fighters in the history of boxing with Jake Paul, a social media personality turned boxer. Tyson, now 60 years old, is a living legend who dominated an entire era of the sport with his ferocity, power, and unmatched skill. Even after decades away from his prime, his name commands unparalleled respect in boxing circles and beyond.
Jake Paul, on the other hand, is a novice in the boxing world, a YouTuber who has used his fame to step into the ring. While he has managed to generate buzz and draw audiences, his fights are more about entertainment than true competitive boxing. Comparing Paul’s achievements to Tyson’s illustrious career is like comparing a casual hobbyist to a master craftsman.
If Tyson ever stepped into the ring with someone like Jake Paul even now it wouldn’t be a fair contest. Tyson’s instinct, technique, and sheer power would make it clear who truly belongs in the realm of boxing greatness. A loss against Tyson even an older Tyson shouldn’t be seen as a blemish for Jake Paul but rather as a reminder that stepping into the ring with a legend is a privilege, not a comparison.
For Jake Paul to be considered a “good boxer,” he would need to face and defeat opponents with real pedigree, not retired MMA fighters or influencers. Until then, his career is a spectacle at best a far cry from the legacy of Mike Tyson, a true titan of the sport.

Title: Michael Knowles REACTS to the Mike Tyson vs. Jake Paul FIGHT
Description: Everyone thinks that the fight between Mike Tyson and Jake Paul was fixed, but nobody agrees why. Either way, some would say ...
Comment:
Arguments against it being fixed:
If they were planning to fix it, they could have made it an exhibition - everyone still would have watched it in the hope of Tyson rolling back the years. There is little point in implementing different rules (heavier gloves, shorter rounds, etc) through a governing body (which is a real PITA) if you're going to fix it anyway. Heavier gloves and shorter rounds do make it easier to avoid a knockout, which could suggest 'fixing', but you could also point to that being a way of ensuring that they could still go normally but with a reduced chance of someone getting hurt.
Tyson still has power but no speed, movement or stamina because he is nearly 60 and has had several health issues - four months ago he was having many blood transfusions. Comment:
Das Problem ist doch wohl eher, es wurde ein Profi Hauptkampf eines International beachteten Kampfsportereignis erwartet und bekommen haben wir noch nicht einmal ein Exhibition. Das ist doch wohl ärgerlich. Ich bin da auch drauf reingefallen. Zum Glück generell ohne Wette.

Außerdem wollen wir im Profi-Boxen einen fairen Kampf sehen. Das war der nie, weil Tyson nicht Kampffähig ist mit dieser Koordination. Außerdem hat die Kommission bis heute nicht eine Dopingkontrolle gemacht.

Was niemand meiner Meinung wirklich beachtet bei den Analysen sind Besonderheiten des Peek A Boo. Das ist noch nicht einmal mein Sport. Beim Training mit Hieb und Stichwaffen und im Vollkontakt ist ebenfalls eine perfekte (!) Koordination bei einer Offensive notwendig. Ansonsten eröffnen sich dem Gegner enorme Möglichkeiten und die wären im realen Kampfgeschehen verehrend.
Das was wir da sehen dürfte aufgrund des Alters auch kein Spaß mehr sein, sollte Jake Paul seine Recht massiv den Kopf treffen.
Der Stil kann sehr Defensiv sein, das sehen wir im weiteren Verlauf. Ich bin sogar erstaunt das er mit diesen Koordinationsproblemen Angriffe wagt. Man sieht auch wie nervös er wird mit der Zeit und am Finger kaut: Der Kopf hat Ideen, sieht Möglichkeiten für massive Angriffe, aber der Instinkt sagt die guten Möglichkeiten des Peek A Boo in der Defensive voll nutzen.
Seine Ecke war auch der Wahnsinn, ihm noch in der Pause zur achten Runde zu raten, massiv in die Offensive zu gehen. Ich frage mich wirklich, ob der Boxtrainer wirklich verstanden hat, was für ein Stil das ist und warum Tyson so reagiert.

Was ich besorgniserregend finde für die Zukunft, dass im Team Tyson anscheinend diese Koordinationsprobleme beim Kampfsparring mit seinem Partner "Magic Mike Russel" nicht aufgefallen sind. Die sind da schon in der Verantwortung für den Alten Kampfsportler, wenn solche Schwierigkeiten auffallen seine Gesundheit zu schützen. Three rounds of combat sparring.

Comment:
The optimism for Tyson was understandable, albeit misguided and delusional for all the reason you mentioned. Had no desire to watch because I didn't want to see Tyson's performance due to my respect for him.

Not to digress, but your education/analysis can be applied to military standards as well. Years ago, I had a coworker who accused the DoD of ageism because he couldn't enlist in the Marine Corps at 40 (this was during the height of combat operations in Iraq and Afghanistan). Using both my common sense and personal experience (medically retired from the USMC at 39), I explained to him their actions weren't due to ageism. Rather, it was based off an understanding of physiology and taking into account the toll military service has on both the mind and body.

To be blunt, you can have all the desire/determination in the world but that doesn't change the fact MC life (as well as the army....not so much Navy or Air Force) can be brutal. In my early-mid 20s, running with my platoon 25-30 miles/week was a breeze. Jumping out the back of a seven-ton with full gear was easy, or patrolling with a 70 lb. pack. But at 39 and with my mileage, I'd metaphorically fold like a lawn chair. This is also why women couldn't serve in combat roles; their bodies simply aren't built to handle basic requirements. I knew a female Marine who could do pull-ups for days and regularly smoked guys on PT runs. All of that is great, but there's no scenario where a 5'4", 130-140 lb. woman can put on a pack half their weight without their legs or back giving out.

First two rounds Tyson was EXPLOSIVE, I really think either the end of the second, or during the third, his knee went from bad to worse. You'll notice he doesn't step in as much, and in boxing stepping in requires a lot of push off with that back leg. He bounces BACK well, which uses the front leg more, but you can see him shuffle strangely after each step back, implying some issues with the knee.

This really feels like, a different day of the week, a different week on a different month where his knee was not giving him as much of an issue we'd have seen at least 2 to 3 more rounds of him maintaining the pace from the first 2, and a more forward moving defense oriented offense during those times he was out of breath. It was obvious he trained a defensive style that would use forward movement, and pretty obvious he couldn't implement it fully.

At least, to anyone who boxed at any point this was obvious.
